answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure, even if Medicaid would typically require prior approval for that service. This applies to situations where Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort after other coverage, including Medicare, has been utilized.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure. This exemption applies even if Medicaid would have otherwise required prior approval for that specific service. This guidance ensures that services already deemed medically necessary and paid for by Medicare do not require a redundant prior approval process from Medicaid.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal regulations mandate that all available resources must be utilized before Medicaid considers payment for services. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization from a primary payer does not negate the primary payers responsibility.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize the principle that Medicaid serves as the payer of last resort, requiring other available coverage to be exhausted first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims when Medicare has already approved and paid for a service, even if Medicaid would typically require it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses more broadly on the federal requirement for providers to pursue all available other coverage before billing Medicaid and notes that failure to obtain prior authorization from a primary payer does not shift responsibility to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is generally not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service or procedure. However, providers must still ensure that all available third-party resources are utilized before billing Medicaid, and failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payer does not shift responsibility to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has approved and paid for a service or procedure. This exemption applies even if Medicaid would typically require prior approval for that specific procedure.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicare crossover claims are explicitly not subject to the requirement of attaching an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) when submitting a claim to Medicaid. This indicates a streamlined process for these types of claims, differing from other third-party claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal and Medicaid regulations mandate that providers pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payor does not negate their responsibility to pay first. Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers must pursue other coverage first, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the primary payors responsibility. This ensures Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to exhaust other coverage options first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both documents agree that failure to obtain prior authorization from a primary payer does not shift the financial responsibility to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update, while not directly addressing prior approval, indicates a simplified process for Medicare crossover claims by exempting them from the Explanation of Benefits attachment requirement [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is generally not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service or procedure. However, providers must still ensure that all available third-party resources are utilized before billing Medicaid, and failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payer does not shift responsibility to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service or procedure. This applies even if Medicaid would ordinarily require prior approval for that specific procedure.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicare crossover claims are not subject to the requirement of attaching an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) when submitting a claim to Medicaid. This indicates a streamlined process for these claims, differing from other third-party claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. The primary payer must pay first, and failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payer does not overcome their responsibility.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal regulations mandate that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers must pursue other coverage first, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the primary payers responsibility.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that providers must utilize all available third-party resources before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources state that failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payer does not shift the responsibility for payment to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service, even if Medicaid would typically require it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update, while addressing Medicare crossover claims, focuses on the exemption from attaching an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) rather than directly discussing prior approval requirements [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
COS 0441 Non bordering state:
Medicaid will accept applications for consideration for limited pharmacy enrollment for pharmacies in a non bordering
state location, when criteria under “Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies” below is met at time of application submission and
when there is an unmet need such as:

• The pharmacy serves a NY Medicaid member that lives in the pharmacy’s service location (e.g., a NY
Medicaid enrolled foster care child, or a NY Medicaid member residing in a NY Medicaid enrolled
residential healthcare facility as defined by Article 28) or
• The pharmacy has an exclusive arrangement to dispense a limited distribution drug on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs found here: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, that is
unavailable from an enrolled pharmacy
Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies (COS 0441, 0161, 0288)
• Follow all federal and NYS laws, regulations, and Medicaid policies.
• Are fully operational, open, and dispensing medications.
• Are fully enrolled in Medicare* as a participating provider.
• Accept Medicare claims assignment and submit Medicare claims for all Medicare covered services on behalf
of a Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill all applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be utilized first. However, if a drug claim is unrelated to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2'.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, meaning providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim, if applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used first. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid, and pharmacies are responsible for coordinating with the proper primary payer, including submitting claims under the appropriate coverage benefit, whether medical or pharmacy.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill all applicable third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Workers' Compensation cases, detailing when Workers' Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for unrelated drug claims with an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides a broader instruction for pharmacies to coordinate with the proper primary payer, including submitting claims under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, without specific mention of Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

This includes coordinating benefits for work-related injuries with Workers' Compensation and ensuring all other responsible payers are included on the claim.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, requiring providers to include all other responsible payers on a claim when applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, providers must utilize Workers' Compensation coverage before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer using an Eligibility Clarification Code of "2" in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will cover the patients responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims, up to the allowed amount.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to coordinate benefits with other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses coordination of benefits for work-related injuries with Workers' Compensation, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx can be billed as primary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits and inquire about other third-party coverage, without detailing specific scenarios like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
COS 0441 Non bordering state:
Medicaid will accept applications for consideration for limited pharmacy enrollment for pharmacies in a non bordering
state location, when criteria under “Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies” below is met at time of application submission and
when there is an unmet need such as:

• The pharmacy serves a NY Medicaid member that lives in the pharmacy’s service location (e.g., a NY
Medicaid enrolled foster care child, or a NY Medicaid member residing in a NY Medicaid enrolled
residential healthcare facility as defined by Article 28) or
• The pharmacy has an exclusive arrangement to dispense a limited distribution drug on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs found here: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, that is
unavailable from an enrolled pharmacy
Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies (COS 0441, 0161, 0288)
• Follow all federal and NYS laws, regulations, and Medicaid policies.
• Are fully operational, open, and dispensing medications.
• Are fully enrolled in Medicare* as a participating provider.
• Accept Medicare claims assignment and submit Medicare claims for all Medicare covered services on behalf
of a Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid requires providers to coordinate benefits by billing all applicable third parties before submitting claims to NYRx Medicaid. This ensures that Medicaid remains the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of "2" in field 309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim if applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, as federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacies must maintain an inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies, such as diabetic supplies, that are applicable to the patients they serve. They must also maintain adequate hours of operation for Medicaid members. Additionally, pharmacies need to be able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Additional information regarding Coordination of Benefits (COB) can be found in several Medicaid Update articles. These articles provide further details on COB submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing. They also include reminders about Medicaid requiring coordination of benefits and pharmacy billing for Medicaid patients with other coverage.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441 must be full service. This means they must dispense and maintain an inventory of commonly dispensed outpatient prescription medications, provide diabetic supplies, and offer immunizations. Community pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche, or that do not immunize, do not meet COS 0441 enrollment criteria.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update provides general information. This document does not contain specific details regarding pharmacy enrollment requirements for outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize that New York State Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require providers to utilize other available coverage before billing NYRx Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance on billing for Workers' Compensation cases, including when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as primary with an Eligibility Clarification Code of "2" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines], whereas the policy update offers a broader statement about billing applicable third parties without specific examples [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual details specific operational requirements for pharmacies, such as maintaining inventory and hours of operation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines], while the policy update refers to other Medicaid Update articles for additional COB information [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].

The provider manual outlines specific enrollment criteria for outpatient dispensing pharmacies under COS 0441, including being full service and providing immunizations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines], a topic not addressed in the provided policy update chunk [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

answers.headline_summary: Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires providers to bill all other liable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. This includes exhausting benefits from sources like Workers' Compensation if applicable, and ensuring all responsible payers are included on the claim.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, necessitating that providers include all other responsible payers on the claim when applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, providers must utilize Workers' Compensation coverage before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid consistently serves as the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate the utilization of all other available resources before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid and should inquire with members about any other third-party coverage they may possess.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to ensure all other responsible payers are billed or included on the claim before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically mentions Workers' Compensation as an example of a third-party payer that must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program for work-related injuries [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits and ask members about other third-party coverage, without specifying Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
COS 0441 Non bordering state:
Medicaid will accept applications for consideration for limited pharmacy enrollment for pharmacies in a non bordering
state location, when criteria under “Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies” below is met at time of application submission and
when there is an unmet need such as:

• The pharmacy serves a NY Medicaid member that lives in the pharmacy’s service location (e.g., a NY
Medicaid enrolled foster care child, or a NY Medicaid member residing in a NY Medicaid enrolled
residential healthcare facility as defined by Article 28) or
• The pharmacy has an exclusive arrangement to dispense a limited distribution drug on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs found here: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, that is
unavailable from an enrolled pharmacy
Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies (COS 0441, 0161, 0288)
• Follow all federal and NYS laws, regulations, and Medicaid policies.
• Are fully operational, open, and dispensing medications.
• Are fully enrolled in Medicare* as a participating provider.
• Accept Medicare claims assignment and submit Medicare claims for all Medicare covered services on behalf
of a Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires providers to bill all other liable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. This includes exhausting all existing benefits and coordinating with primary payers, such as Workers' Compensation, before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, requiring providers to include all other responsible payers on a claim when applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, providers must utilize Workers' Compensation coverage before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is unrelated to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid is consistently the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid. They should also inquire about other third-party coverage from members to ensure proper Coordination of Benefits.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to utilize all other available resources and exhaust existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Workers' Compensation coverage for work-related injuries, detailing when it must be utilized and providing an exception for unrelated claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers should ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper COB, without specifying particular types of coverage like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_36.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying the validity of prescriptions and the prescribers identity, and for using professional judgment in emergency situations. They must also notify prescribers when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If certain patient information, such as address, age, or CIN, is missing, the pharmacist is not always required to enter it. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the patient, or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. However, controlled substance prescriptions must adhere to Article 33 requirements, and pharmacists can only modify specific information.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary regarding any changes. This policy is less specific about missing patient information compared to the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider manual outlines general responsibilities for pharmacists, including patient counseling and record retention. Pharmacists must ensure counseling is consistent with federal and state law, and records must be retained for six years. Delivery confirmation must also be maintained for six years, and Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must inform the prescriber that PA is needed and notify the patient once it is obtained. If the prescriber cannot be reached, the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit for PA or enter agreements to effectuate PAs, except for emergency supplies or when employed by a prescriber.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of a prescription and the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown. Electronic imaging of prescriptions must be an exact reproduction of the original order. Authentication may be required for these electronic images.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies are responsible for using professional judgment when filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if they were written during an emergency department visit and the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions. This policy specifically addresses emergency situations where prescriber verification might be delayed.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to ensure prescription legitimacy and patient safety when information is missing or unclear [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual details specific missing patient information, such as address, age, or CIN, and the conditions under which pharmacists are not required to enter it, while the policy focuses on verifying prescription directions for use [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The policy provides specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities regarding prior authorization for non-preferred drugs when prescriber information is missing, including initiating a 72-hour emergency supply, which is not detailed in the provided provider manual chunk [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The policy explicitly addresses the use of professional judgment in emergency department situations when the assigned PCP is unavailable, requiring follow-up the next business day, a scenario not directly covered in the provided provider manual chunk [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

answers.headline_summary: When patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying the validity of prescriptions and the prescribers identity, especially if the prescriber is unknown. For missing patient details like address, age, or CIN, pharmacists can proceed if the information is readily available in pharmacy records or if they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid members address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must adhere to Article 33 of the Public Health Law, and pharmacists can only add or change specific information on them as per Title 10, Part 80.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use. They should also consult the prescriber as necessary regarding any changes. This policy statement is broader and less specific than the provider manual regarding missing patient information.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescribers identity. This is particularly important if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions must be an exact reproduction and may require authentication.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a non-preferred drug prescription requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must inform the prescriber that PA is needed. If the prescriber cannot be reached, the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit for PAs or enter agreements with prescribers to effectuate PAs, except for the 72-hour emergency supply or when employed by a prescriber.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists role in ensuring prescription legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual details specific scenarios for missing patient information like address, age, or CIN, outlining when a pharmacist can proceed without entering this data [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy focuses on the pharmacists responsibilities when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, including notifying the prescriber and the limited ability to initiate a 72-hour emergency supply PA [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

This policy is less specific as it broadly refers to providers and does not detail the process for verifying prescriber identity or the handling of electronic prescription images.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to ensure prescription legitimacy and accuracy when information is missing or unclear [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual specifies conditions under which pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient demographic information, while the policy focuses on the use of professional judgment in emergency situations when a PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual details the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity and the exact reproduction of electronic prescriptions, whereas the policy broadly advises providers to verify directions for use and consult prescribers on changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

test_id_39.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_39.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying the validity of prescriptions and the prescribers identity, and for using professional judgment in emergency situations. They must also notify prescribers when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Prescriptions for controlled substances must adhere to Article 33 of the Public Health Law. Pharmacists are permitted to add or change only specific information on controlled substance prescriptions, as outlined in Title 10, Part 80.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a non-preferred drug prescription requires prior authorization, the pharmacist must inform the prescriber and the patient once prior authorization is obtained. If the prescriber cannot be reached, the pharmacist may initiate a prior authorization for a 72-hour emergency supply. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit prior authorizations or enter agreements with prescribers for prior authorizations and appeals.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists must make a good faith effort to verify the prescription's validity and the prescriber's identity if the prescriber is unknown. If patient information such as address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if it is readily available in pharmacy records. This also applies if the pharmacist knows the person or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The pharmacy is responsible for exercising professional judgment when filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if they originated from an emergency department visit and the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions. This policy is less specific than the provider manual regarding general missing information.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update address pharmacist responsibilities when certain information is missing or specific conditions apply to prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual details pharmacist responsibilities for verifying general prescription validity and handling missing patient information, while the policy update focuses on the pharmacists role in emergency situations and prior authorization for non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual specifies requirements for controlled substance prescriptions, whereas the policy update outlines procedures for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

They must also notify prescribers when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid members address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information if it is readily available in pharmacy records. This also applies if the pharmacist knows the person or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. aligned_pairs.policy_update: The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment when filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if they were written as part of an emergency department visit. This applies if the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to exercise professional judgment regarding prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both documents highlight the importance of verifying prescription details and, if needed, consulting with the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
