test_id_41.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, anorexia, or weight gain, as well as for cosmetic purposes or sexual dysfunction, in accordance with federal and state legislation. This exclusion applies to both commercially available drugs and ingredients used in compounded medications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being created for indications excluded by NYS Medicaid. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs for weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes. These exclusions are mandated by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be formulated for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This prohibition is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Examples of such excluded indications include drugs for weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not provide reimbursement for drugs prescribed for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion also extends to drugs used for cosmetic purposes or hair growth. These limitations are established pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs utilized in the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. Additionally, drugs intended for cosmetic use or hair growth are not reimbursable. These exclusions are consistent with policy and/or state or federal legislation, specifically SSA §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, such as weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both documents explicitly exclude reimbursement for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources confirm that drugs for cosmetic use or hair growth are not reimbursable under SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, anorexia, or weight gain, as well as for cosmetic purposes or sexual dysfunction, in accordance with federal and state legislation. This exclusion applies to both commercially available drugs and ingredients used in compounded medications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for indications excluded by NYS Medicaid. This includes drugs intended to treat weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or for cosmetic purposes, as specified by Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Reimbursement for ingredients in a compound claim is only provided if they are on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable Drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be created for indications excluded by NYS Medicaid. This aligns with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), which specifically lists drugs for weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes as examples of excluded indications. The policy emphasizes that these restrictions apply to compounds.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not provide reimbursement for drugs used in the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is in accordance with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Additionally, drugs for cosmetic use or hair growth are not reimbursable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs that are used to treat anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This policy is consistent with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Furthermore, drugs intended for cosmetic use or hair growth are also not reimbursable.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy explicitly state that compounded medications cannot be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes, as per Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both documents confirm that drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable by Medicaid, citing Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both the provider manual and policy agree that drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth are not reimbursable [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, explicitly mentioning weight loss drugs as an example [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both documents specify that drugs for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable by Medicaid, citing SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

test_id_45.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, anorexia, or weight gain, as well as for cosmetic purposes or sexual dysfunction, in accordance with federal and state legislation. This exclusion applies to both commercially available drugs and ingredients used in compounded medications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications are not permitted to contain excluded drugs or be created for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. These exclusions include, but are not limited to, drugs for weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes. This policy is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs for weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes. This policy aligns with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not provide reimbursement for drugs used in the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. Additionally, drugs for cosmetic use or hair growth are not reimbursed. These exclusions are pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used to treat anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion also applies to drugs for cosmetic use or hair growth. These policies are in accordance with state or federal legislation, specifically SSA §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy explicitly state that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, such as weight loss, sexual dysfunction, or cosmetic purposes, referencing the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that drugs for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, weight gain, cosmetic use, or hair growth are not reimbursable by Medicaid, citing SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format

Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost. Claims must include specific identifiers and the 340B acquisition cost in designated fields to avoid duplicate discounts and ensure proper reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost. This reimbursement also includes a professional dispensing fee of $10.18. This applies when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for Fee-for-Service members. This cost should be net of any manufacturer discounts or other price reductions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts for 340B drugs. New York Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to prevent these duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove them from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Drugs obtained through the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service-only claims. This ensures proper identification for reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims. Additionally, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost and Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers are required.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format require specific fields. These fields include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost for primary claims. The Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) must be reported as the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources require specific NCPDP fields for 340B drug claims, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6].
differences: The provider manual explicitly mentions a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 in addition to the 340B acquisition cost, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies the use of the UD modifier for identifying 340B drugs on Fee-for-Service-only claims, while the provider manual broadly refers to '340B claim level identifiers' to avoid duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost, with specific claim identifiers required to prevent duplicate discounts. The reimbursement process involves submitting the 340B acquisition cost in designated fields on NCPDP claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost. This reimbursement also includes a professional dispensing fee of $10.18, provided the drug meets the definition of a Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at Usual and Customary (U&C).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for fee-for-service members. This cost should be net of any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: New York Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts. Federal law prohibits manufacturers from providing both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove them from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted New York State Medicaid fee-for-service-only claims to ensure reimbursement. Medicare requires JG or TB modifiers for crossover claims, but the UD modifier is not needed for these claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost for primary claims. Additionally, the Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) must be reported as the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format require specific fields such as SCC 20, BCD 08 for primary claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost for primary claims. The Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) must also be reported as the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers. Claims with incorrect data are subject to audit and recovery.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources emphasize the importance of using specific claim identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both the provider manual and policy update specify the exact NCPDP fields and codes required for submitting 340B drug claims, including SCC 20, BCD 08, and reporting the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6].
differences: The provider manual explicitly mentions a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B drugs, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies the use of the UD modifier for 340B drug lines on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, while the provider manual discusses claim level identifiers more broadly without naming a specific modifier [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy update notes that Medicare requires JG or TB modifiers for crossover claims, a detail not present in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

test_id_51.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in the NYS Medicaid Program; instead, they must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit. Pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party, including Medicaid, and must use the NCPDP transaction format for claims not billed to a facility.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party, which may include Medicaid. Transactions must be submitted through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program using the NCPDP transaction format when not billing a facility.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid allows certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require administration by a healthcare provider to be covered under both the medical and pharmacy benefits. Eligibility for pharmacy benefit coverage requires the drug to be billable in NCPDP format, deliverable to the place of administration, not included in a facilitys rate, and available from a Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioners office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia. Specific policies and billing guidance for certain drugs and drug classes are available on the DOH website.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy indicate that home infusion drugs are covered by NYS Medicaid, though through different mechanisms [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring billing as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, a detail not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual outlines specific criteria for home infusion drugs to be covered under the pharmacy benefit, such as NCPDP billing format and delivery to the place of administration, which is more detailed than the policys general statement on practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy update emphasizes the pharmacy's responsibility for timely billing and the use of the NCPDP transaction format for claims not billed to a facility, which is not explicitly detailed in the provider manual's discussion of home infusion coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
