answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in NYS Medicaid but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. These drugs, including those requiring practitioner administration, are eligible for reimbursement when specific clinical criteria and billing guidelines are met, often requiring submission via the NCPDP format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as either a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies that are not required to bill a facility must submit transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. This submission must use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid allows certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require practitioner administration to be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits. These drugs must meet specific criteria, such as being billable in NCPDP format and deliverable to the place of administration. Additionally, the drug must not be included in a facility rate and must be available from a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office. Coverage is for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia. An approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol is required in the absence of a recognized indication.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy indicate that home infusion drugs are eligible for reimbursement under specific conditions.

Both sources acknowledge that drugs requiring practitioner administration are covered by NYS Medicaid when certain criteria are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion treatments are not covered by a bundled payment, requiring separate billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit, which is not directly addressed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies that drugs administered in a practitioners office are covered for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia, or with an approved IRB protocol, which is a more detailed clinical criterion than the general requirements in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual details specific criteria for pharmacy benefit coverage of practitioner-administered drugs, such as NCPDP billing format and delivery to the place of administration, which are not mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_53.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in NYS Medicaid but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. These drugs, including those requiring practitioner administration, are eligible for reimbursement when specific clinical criteria and billing guidelines are met, often requiring submission via the NCPDP format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as either a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies not required to bill a facility must submit transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. These submissions must use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid allows certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require practitioner administration to be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits. These drugs must meet specific criteria, such as being billable in NCPDP format, deliverable to the place of administration, and not included in a facilitys rate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia. Coverage is also provided with an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy acknowledge that drugs requiring practitioner administration, which can include home infusion drugs, are eligible for coverage under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both documents implicitly support the need for specific billing formats or criteria for drug reimbursement, with the policy explicitly mentioning the NCPDP format for pharmacy transactions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion treatments are not covered by a bundled payment and require separate billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit, a detail not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on the coverage criteria for practitioner-administered drugs based on FDA approval, compendia support, or IRB protocols, while the provider manual details operational criteria for pharmacy dispensing, such as NCPDP billing capability and direct delivery to the administration place [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in NYS Medicaid but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. These drugs, including those requiring practitioner administration, are eligible for reimbursement when specific clinical criteria and billing guidelines are met, often requiring submission via the NCPDP format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Providers can access a list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit online.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies that are not required to bill a facility must submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. This submission must use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as a facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid allows certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require practitioner administration to be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits. These drugs must meet specific criteria, such as being billable in the NCPDP format and deliverable directly to the place of administration. Additionally, the drug must not be included in a facilitys rate and must be available from a Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office. Coverage is for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia. An approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol is required in the absence of a recognized indication, with documentation maintained in the patients clinical file.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update indicate that drugs requiring practitioner administration, including home infusion drugs, are eligible for Medicaid coverage under specific conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both documents emphasize the importance of proper billing procedures for drugs, with the provider manual specifying separate billing for home infusion drugs and the policy detailing the NCPDP format for pharmacy submissions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring separate billing for drugs and supplies, while the policy update focuses on the general requirement for pharmacies to use the NCPDP format for billing when not billing a facility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual details specific criteria for pharmacy dispensing of practitioner-administered drugs, such as the ability to be billed in NCPDP format and direct delivery to the administration site, whereas the policy update focuses on medical necessity criteria like FDA approval or compendia support for administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone reimbursement involves separate billing for the drug and its administration. The pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication via National Drug Code (NDC) using a specific methodology, and for administration using procedure code 96372 with an administration fee. Policy documents, while not directly addressing naloxone, discuss general reimbursement for services by Managed Care Plans (MMCPs) based on units or hours over a 60-day period.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For naloxone, claims for the drug and its administration must be submitted separately. The pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the ordering prescriber's NPI. For administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy's NPI, and the pharmacy will receive the current administration fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Managed Care Plans (MMCPs) will reimburse for an initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service. This reimbursement covers a period of 60 days from the time the MMCP receives notification from the Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) provider. This policy does not specifically address naloxone reimbursement.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy documents discuss reimbursement for services, although the policy document provides a general example rather than specific guidance on naloxone.
differences: The provider manual offers specific instructions for naloxone reimbursement, detailing separate billing for the drug and administration using NDCs and procedure code 96372, while the policy document describes general service reimbursement by Managed Care Plans based on units or hours over a 60-day period [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

aligned_pairs.policy_update: Reimbursement for the cost of vaccines for members 19 years of age and older is made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy. answers.caveats: The policy document provides a general example of pharmacy reimbursement for vaccines, which is a partial match to the specific details of naloxone reimbursement outlined in the provider manual.

prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone reimbursement involves separate billing for the drug and its administration. The pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication via NDC using the ordering prescriber's NPI, and for administration using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy's NPI. Policy documents, however, do not specifically detail naloxone reimbursement but rather general service authorization and vaccine reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: When billing for naloxone, claims for the drug and its administration must be submitted separately. The drug is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the ordering prescriber's National Provider Identifier (NPI). Administration, billed with procedure code 96372, is reimbursed at the current administration fee using the pharmacy's NPI.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Reimbursement for the cost of vaccines for members 19 years of age and older will be made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy. This policy statement provides a general example of pharmacy reimbursement for a specific drug type, which is the closest available policy match for drug reimbursement methodology.

differences: The provider manual explicitly details the billing process for naloxone, including separate claims for the drug and its administration, whereas the policy document discusses reimbursement for vaccines [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

The provider manual specifies the use of NDC for drug reimbursement and procedure code 96372 for administration, along with the respective NPIs for the prescriber and pharmacy, while the policy document broadly states reimbursement for vaccines at no more than the actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
